# Diagnose the Material

Start by naming what you are actually auditing. The diagnosis changes the standard you use.

## 1. Identify the artifact

- **Whole outline / chapter run:** Audit chapter function, sequencing, escalation, climax load, aftermath length, and seam behavior.
- **Single chapter plan:** Audit internal rhythm, scene order, chapter opening/closing pressure, and whether the chapter repeats neighboring work.
- **Scene plan:** Audit goal/conflict/turn/exit value and whether the scene earns its length.
- **Prose chapter:** Ignore sentence-level style at first. Read for scene boundaries, pressure changes, sequel density, revelations, and chapter-end propulsion.

## 2. Name the scale of the complaint

Figure out whether the user means:

- **Local drag** — one scene or chapter stalls
- **Run-level plateau** — several adjacent chapters do the same kind of work
- **Act-shape problem** — the middle sags, the late act stretches, the climax underdelivers, the coda sprawls
- **Contrast problem** — everything is loud, or everything is quiet, so nothing lands

## 3. Name the story phase

This matters because the same beat can be healthy in one zone and weak in another.

- **Setup / early act:** room for orientation, promise, world texture, and slower trust-building
- **Middle build:** pressure should branch, recombine, and keep changing what the cast knows or wants
- **Late act / endgame:** scene density usually increases, sequel space tightens, and duplicated chapter work becomes more expensive
- **Climax:** mostly live-room action, confrontation, or decision pressure
- **Aftermath / coda:** shorter, more selective, and purposeful rather than sprawling

## 4. Identify what "flat" means here

Do not settle for "the pace feels off." Name the exact failure:

- same value shift repeated
- same chapter function repeated
- all sequel, no fresh pressure
- all escalation, no contrast
- travel with no new cost or intelligence
- aftermath restating what the climax already said
- coda doing more than one job

## 5. Keep one caution in view

Not every quiet chapter is drag. A stillness chapter can earn itself if it changes the relationship, reframes the threat, builds the next plan, or gives the reader contrast before the sprint.
